Title: Right-size the model to the task
Body: Don't send every request to your most expensive model. Route simple classification/extraction to a small/cheap (or local) model and reserve frontier models for hard reasoning and coding. This is the single biggest cost lever.

Title: Cache stable prefixes
Body: If a large system prompt or document repeats across calls, use prompt caching. The cached portion is processed once and re-billed at a steep discount, cutting both cost and latency on long contexts.

Title: Retrieve, don't stuff
Body: Instead of pasting an entire codebase or knowledge base, use RAG to pull only the few most relevant chunks. Smaller, focused context is cheaper AND usually produces better answers than a giant dump.

Title: Trim and summarize history
Body: In long chats/agents, old turns silently eat your context window and money. Periodically summarize or drop stale history. In Claude Code, /clear or compaction between unrelated tasks keeps headroom high.

Title: Cap the output
Body: Output tokens often cost more than input tokens. Ask for concise answers, set max_tokens, and request only the format you need (e.g. 'JSON only, no prose') to avoid paying for filler.

Title: Caveman prompts save tokens
Body: Terse, imperative instructions ('Read file. Find bug. Patch. Output diff only.') often match polite, verbose prompts in quality while using fewer tokens - especially for repeated automation prompts.

Title: Prefer loops over one perfect prompt
Body: For multi-step work, an agentic loop (act -> observe -> retry) beats agonizing over a single mega-prompt. Let the model run tests and self-correct rather than trying to specify everything up front.

Title: Give agents project memory
Body: A CLAUDE.md / rules file (or equivalent) stops an agent from re-discovering the same project facts every session - saving tokens and avoiding mistakes. Capture conventions, commands and gotchas once.

Title: Measure before optimizing
Body: Use usage logs (or a gateway like LiteLLM) to find which prompts actually burn the most tokens. Optimize those first instead of guessing - most spend usually concentrates in a few hot paths.

Title: Lower temperature for facts
Body: For factual/deterministic tasks, set a low temperature and ask for sources to reduce hallucination. Save higher temperature for brainstorming and creative work.
